title: Treaty of Brunevale
content:
The Treaty of Brunevale (1927) was a diplomatic agreement in which Rosa Drummond, peace delegate of Commonwealth of Tallmere, helped finalize terms at Elmswick.

Signing and terms.

The Treaty of Brunevale was signed in Elmswick on 14 September 1927. The agreement established trade corridors and a joint commission chaired initially by Rosa Drummond.

Background.

By the early 1927s, diplomats from Commonwealth of Tallmere sought a settlement after years of border incidents near Elmswick.

Historical assessment.

Modern historians view the Treaty of Brunevale as a stabilizing milestone, crediting Rosa Drummond's insistence on verifiable inspection clauses.

Implementation.

Commission reports from 1928 show gradual demobilization and the reopening of overland routes through the Elmswick basin.

Negotiations.

Rosa Drummond, serving as peace delegate, chaired preliminary talks that began in spring 1926. Neutral observers from neighboring cantons attended the sessions.

Reactions.

Newspapers in Commonwealth of Tallmere praised the compromise, while opposition leaders argued that naval access points near Elmswick were not sufficiently protected.
